records.ety: [OE. spurnen to kick against, to stumble over, AS. spurnan to kick, offend; akin to spura spur, OS. & OHG. spurnan to kick, Icel. spyrna, L. spernere to despise, Skr. sphur to jerk, to push. √171. See Spur.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Spurned (sprnd); p. pr. & vb. n. Spurning.]

defs: To drive back or away, as with the foot; to kick.
quotes: [The bird] with his foot will spurn adown his cup.
I spurn thee like a cur out of my way.

defs: To reject with disdain; to scorn to receive or accept; to treat with contempt.
quotes: What safe and nicely I might well delay By rule of knighthood, I disdain and spurn.
Domestics will pay a more cheerful service when they find themselves not spurned because fortune has laid them at their master's feet.

defs: To kick or toss up the heels.
quotes: The miller spurned at a stone.
The drunken chairman in the kennel spurns.

defs: To manifest disdain in rejecting anything; to make contemptuous opposition or resistance.
quotes: Nay, more, to spurn at your most royal image.

defs: A kick; a blow with the foot.
quotes: What defense can properly be used in such a despicable encounter as this but either the slap or the spurn?
The insolence of office, and the spurns That patient merit of the unworthy takes.

defs: A body of coal left to sustain an overhanging mass.
